Name: information_theoretic_pruning
Title: Information-Theoretic Neural Network Pruning: Operational Bounds on Bits-Per-Weight for Predictive Sufficiency
Short Hypothesis: Pruning neural networks can be framed as a lossy source coding problem, where the goal is to minimize the bits-per-weight while preserving predictive sufficiency, and operational bounds on this trade-off can be derived using mutual information and entropy estimators.
Related Work: Existing pruning methods focus on empirical heuristics or hardware-specific optimizations, often lacking a theoretical foundation. Recent work has explored information-theoretic frameworks for generalization and compression, but operational bounds on bits-per-weight for pruning remain unexplored. This proposal distinguishes itself by deriving achievable bounds on the bits-per-weight needed for predictive sufficiency, leveraging mutual information and entropy estimators.
Abstract: Neural network pruning is a widely used technique for model compression, but existing methods often rely on empirical heuristics without a theoretical foundation. This paper proposes an information-theoretic framework for pruning, framing it as a lossy source coding problem under a distortion constraint. We derive operational bounds on the bits-per-weight needed to preserve predictive sufficiency, using mutual information and entropy estimators to quantify the trade-off between model size and task performance. Our approach provides a principled way to allocate bits where they reduce task-relevant distortion most, bridging the gap between theoretical bounds and practical implementations. Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art compression rates while maintaining predictive performance, offering a new perspective on neural network pruning.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed pruning method on benchmark datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) using mutual information estimators (e.g., InfoNCE) to quantify task-relevant information.
Compare the bits-per-weight achieved by our method to existing pruning techniques, using predictive accuracy as the distortion measure.
Analyze the trade-off between model size and task performance by varying the distortion constraint and plotting the resulting rate-distortion curves.
Validate the derived operational bounds by comparing empirical results to theoretical predictions, assessing the tightness of the bounds.
Investigate the robustness of the method to different architectures (e.g., ResNet, VGG) and tasks (e.g., classification, regression).
Risk Factors and Limitations: The mutual information estimators used may have high variance, leading to unstable bounds.
The theoretical bounds may not be tight enough to guide practical pruning decisions.
The method may not generalize well to tasks with complex dependencies or high-dimensional data.
The computational cost of estimating mutual information could be prohibitive for large models.
